payload.claim_text: By charging vehicles a toll to enter the Congestion Relief Zone, defined as local streets and avenues in Manhattan at or below 60th Street, Congestion Pricing will encourage drivers to switch to transit (subways, buses, and railroads) into the Congestion Relief Zone and avoid the toll.
payload.subject: Congestion Pricing

payload.claim_text: E-Hail is a premium service for people with disabilities eligible for the MTA's Access-A-Ride paratransit program. It provides more flexibility than dedicated or non-dedicated (i.e. broker) Access-A-Ride service by enabling customers to book taxi or for-hire vehicle (FHV) rides directly.
payload.subject: E-Hail program

records.display_name: S&B TVMs/TOMs Aging and PCI Compliance

payload.claim_text: The current fare collection TVMs/TOMs were first installed by S&B in year 2000. Over time, hardware components have been refurbished and upgraded multiple times to keep these obsolete ticketing machines operational. By year 2025, S&B will be unable to maintain the same level of support. TVMs/TOMs represent 30 percent of the Railroads' ticket sales with 99 percent availability.
payload.subject: Scheidt & Bachmann TVMs/TOMs

payload.claim_text: MTA and TBTA currently have $3.73 billion of outstanding Build America Bonds ('BABs'). The 35% cash subsidy payments on the BABs have been significantly reduced since 2013 through federal budget sequestration increasing the net effective interest cost of the taxable BABs.
payload.subject: Build America Bonds

records.display_name: Railroad Fare Systems Past Service Life

payload.claim_text: The current fare collection systems in use by the Railroads were installed almost 25 years ago and are well-past their expected service life. These systems cannot be maintained in a state of good repair beyond 2025.
payload.subject: LIRR and MNR fare collection systems

payload.claim_text: Substantial Completion of the Contract can be achieved by December 2025, with better functionality and a substantial savings in delay costs.
payload.subject: Cubic OMNY Contract

records.display_name: Masabi Software Critical to Fare Operations

payload.claim_text: Since the 2016 launch of mobile ticketing, Masabi's software has become critical to Railroad fare collection operations.
payload.subject: Masabi mobile ticketing software

description: Contactless fare payment system launched in 2019 to replace MetroCards. By end of 2024, 60% of transit riders used OMNY. Anticipates completing rollout and retiring MetroCards by 2026. Includes automatic fare-capping.

Account-based, open-payment system allowing customers to pay fares using mobile apps, digital wallets, contactless bank cards, and MTA-issued contactless transit cards. Includes Configurable Vending Machine (CVM) deployment and software/hardware enhancements for Metro-North, LIRR, and NYC Transit.
New fare payment system with contactless payments, fare-capping, retail locations, configurable vending machines, and commuter railroad implementation.
Maintenance of communications network infrastructure to support the implementation of OMNY, NYCT's new fare payment platform.
Multi-year design-build program replacing MetroCard with contactless fare payment. Scope includes NYCT subway/bus, Commuter Railroads, and affiliates.
OMNY fare payment system for LIRR and MNR, originally targeted to replace mobile ticketing by mid-2025, now expected to complete by 2027
Modification to Cubic contract A34024 to remove LIRR/MNR requirements, add B2B Portal and mobile app enhancements, and extend schedule to December 2025.
Software support to further integrate OMNY at NYCT
Expansion of OMNY fare payment system to the Paratransit program

Transition to contactless fare payment system. Over 1 million reduced-fare OMNY cards distributed; remaining 500,000 expected within weeks. Recent agreements ensuring OMNY available on NICE Bus in Nassau County. Additional card vending machines installed.
Multi-year design-build program replacing MetroCard with OMNY contactless fare collection system, including CVMs, OMNY validators, and closed-loop card distribution
Modification to provide additional capabilities to the existing OMNY point-of-sale network, reduced fare and paratransit functions, and technical support through July 31, 2030

MTA's transition from MetroCard to OMNY. Over 670 OMNY vending machines deployed at more than 325 subway stations. All student and reduced-fare programs transitioned to OMNY. Completion of installation of 980 vending machines by Labor Day 2025. MetroCard sales to cease December 31, 2025.
Contactless fare payment system under One Metro NY (OMNY) brand, replacing legacy payment systems for NYC Transit and MTA Bus Company
MTA's transition from MetroCard to OMNY fare payment system; includes vending machine installation, customer segment migration, and railroad integration
Transitioning to OMNY - Increase Appeal for Customers

Modification to contract with Cubic Transportation Systems for New Fare Payment System (OMNY) to procure 30 Customer Service Point of Sale (CS POS) Terminals. 24 front office, 2 back office, 4 mobile. 26 Production, 4 Stage devices. Amount $1,364,510.
Substantial completion of the OMNY fare payment system in 2025, achieving 98 percent adoption following the sunset of the MetroCard.
Substantial completion of the OMNY fare payment system in 2025, achieving 98 percent adoption following the sunset of the MetroCard

Contactless fare payment system. Capital program reached substantial completion at end of 2025, returning more than $60M to capital program, adoption rate exceeding 98 percent, processing approximately six million taps per day.
New fare payment system replacing MetroCard and legacy LIRR/MNR systems

Modification to contract A-34024 to procure additional OMNY Extended-Use and Limited-Use media for 2026, including EU Media (7,402,000), LU FanFold Media (10,540,000), and LU Die-Cut Media (6,509,800).
OMNY is MTA's new fare payment system. OMNY will eventually combine fare payments and mobile ticketing across subways, buses, and commuter rail. MTA expects to roll out OMNY between 2019 and 2023.

One Metro New York (OMNY) is a D/B project replacing MTA's Subway, Bus and Commuter Railroad fare systems. Open architecture account-based system accepting contactless credit/debit cards, mobile payment, and MTA issued contactless cards. Current budget $772M, $346M expended, 45% complete.

OMNY fare capping pilot launched in March 2022. After 12 OMNY trips in a calendar week with the same device, rides are free for the rest of the week (Lucky 13). Benefitted 76,000 customers in first two weeks.

OMNY weekly fare capping pilot beginning February 28, 2022. Customers ride free for rest of week after 12 subway or local bus trips using same card/device Monday-Sunday. Part of MTA Best Fares program.

Contactless fare payment system. In June 2022, 37 million OMNY taps representing 32% of NYCT fare revenue. OMNY has processed transactions from bank cards issued in 179 countries. Introduction of 'Best Fare' weekly fare capping in March 2022.

Modification to contract A-86071 with CH2M Hill New York, Inc. to add three-year extension (Nov 1, 2022-Oct 31, 2025) and $13,482,418 for consulting services supporting NYC Transit's New Fare Payment System (OMNY).
Contactless open-loop fare payment system replacing MetroCard. System integrator is Cubic Transportation Systems. Contract awarded November 2017.
